coredata.dc:title: Turn-off chelation-enhanced fluorescence sensing of carbon dot-metallic deep eutectic solvent by imidazole-based small molecules
coredata.dc:creator: Manshaei, Faranak
Bagheri, Habib
Es-haghi, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Low accuracy, requirement of expensive instruments, derivatization and multi-step sample preparation, inconsistencies during the fabrication process, time-consuming procedures and utilization of eco-unfriendly chemicals are some limitations of various developed methods for detection of 4-methylimidazol (4-MeI). Herein, a rapid and miniaturized fluorescent probe based on the ultrasound‒assisted formation of carbon dots (CDs) in a metallic deep eutectic solvent (MDES) was developed and conveniently implemented to detect 4-MeI in commercial dark soft drinks. In-situ synthesis of CDs-chelated metals in the presence of the target analyte facilitates a kinetically fluorescence emission with no need to multi-step analysis. Simultaneous implementation of MDES as a green solvent and passivation agent also practically paves the way to minimize the hazardous chemicals consumption. Chelation of the divalent metal cations on the synthesized CDs and their strong interactions with the imidazole ring presumably provides a fluorescence quenching pathway induced by the model species. The formation kinetics of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI (as the model compound) and caffeine (as the significant interference) were also studied under the optimum conditions. Accordingly, the calibration plot covers 0.6−320 ng mL−1 concentration range. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) are 0.18 and 0.6 ng mL−1, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the developed method are <11 % and the relative recoveries for eight real samples are in the range of 89–108 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Homogeneous fluorescence assay
Carbon dots
Metallic deep eutectic solvents
Miniaturized detection method
Sensing mechanism
Kinetic studies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-situ synthesis of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI and MDES medium
Synthesis and characterization of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI and MDES
Formation kinetics of CDs in different solvents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Razi Vaccine and Serum Research Institute

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Research Council (Grant number QA990417 ) and Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology (SUT) and Razi Vaccine & Serum Research Institute are acknowledged for supporting this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesized metallic deep eutectic solvents with different metal salts. a) NiCl2.6H2O, b) ZnCl2. 4H2O, c) CoCl2. 6H2O, d) CuCl2.2H2O.
The schematic of in-situ synthesis of CDs in the presence of the 4-MeI and MDES.
The NMR spectra of Cu-DES (green) and DES (red) (a). The FT-IR spectra of choline chloride (black), ethylene glycol (red) and Cu-DES (blue) (b).

The FT-IR spectra of Cu-DES (blue), citric acid (orange), synthesized CDS (corn) (a). The size distribution analysis of the synthesized CDS by DLS (b). The absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (blue) and emission (red) spectra of the synthesized CDS (c). The fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDS at different λex (d).

Optimization data achieved to compare the performance of solvent type and passivation agent (a), citric acid mass (b), pH (c), solvent amount (d), sonication amplitude (e), sonication time (f). The bar charts and dotted line represent the analytical response (I0/I) and the intrinsic fluorescence intensity (relative fluorescence units, RFU), respectively.

The formation kinetic studies of CDS in different solvents (Cu-MDES, ethanol and water) and presence of 4-MeI versus the sonication time (a-c). Comparison of the analytical response of 4-MeI with various interferences (100 times concentrated) and investigation of selectivity (d). The formation kinetic studies of CDS in different solvents (Cu-MDES, ethanol and water) and presence of caffeine versus the sonication time (e-g). The blue/red solid and dotted lines represent the analytical response (I0/I) and the intrinsic fluorescence intensity (relative fluorescence units, RFU), respectively.

Fluorescence emission spectra (a) and Stern-Volmer plot (b) of the synthesized sensor in the presence of different concentrations of 4-MeI. The inset shows the UV–vis spectra of CDs before and after addition of 4-MeI at concentration level of 20 ng mL−1.
Analytical parameters of the method for determination 4-MeI.
Relative recoveries of 4-MeI calculated from different real samples.
Comparison of the proficiency of present method with other relevant methods, employed for 4-MeI.

row.entry: Fluorescence microplate assay based on CDS/MDES

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Solid phase extraction-Liquid chromatography- Tandem mass spectrometry.

head.ce:title: Turn-off chelation-enhanced fluorescence sensing of carbon dot-metallic deep eutectic solvent by imidazole-based small molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physico Chemistry, Razi Vaccine & Serum Research Institute, Agricultural Research, Education and Extension Organization (AREEO), P.O. Box 31975/148, Karaj, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Research, Education and Extension Organization (AREEO)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physico Chemistry, Razi Vaccine & Serum Research Institute, Agricultural Research, Education and Extension Organization (AREEO), P.O. Box 31975/148, Karaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low accuracy, requirement of expensive instruments, derivatization and multi-step sample preparation, inconsistencies during the fabrication process, time-consuming procedures and utilization of eco-unfriendly chemicals are some limitations of various developed methods for detection of 4-methylimidazol (4-MeI). Herein, a rapid and miniaturized fluorescent probe based on the ultrasound‒assisted formation of carbon dots (CDs) in a metallic deep eutectic solvent (MDES) was developed and conveniently implemented to detect 4-MeI in commercial dark soft drinks. In-situ synthesis of CDs-chelated metals in the presence of the target analyte facilitates a kinetically fluorescence emission with no need to multi-step analysis. Simultaneous implementation of MDES as a green solvent and passivation agent also practically paves the way to minimize the hazardous chemicals consumption. Chelation of the divalent metal cations on the synthesized CDs and their strong interactions with the imidazole ring presumably provides a fluorescence quenching pathway induced by the model species. The formation kinetics of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI (as the model compound) and caffeine (as the significant interference) were also studied under the optimum conditions. Accordingly, the calibration plot covers 0.6−320 ng mL−1 concentration range. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) are 0.18 and 0.6 ng mL−1, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the developed method are <11 % and the relative recoveries for eight real samples are in the range of 89–108 %.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) have attracted researchers' attention due to their tunable emission properties, good biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, and simple surface functionalization [1]. The simple synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using inexpensive and eco-friendly carbon sources and functional agents is their additional favorable properties [2]. Various pathways have been developed regarding the synthesis of CDs, including pyrolysis [3], microwave treatment [4], electrochemical [5], hydrothermal [6], and ultrasound-assisted [7] methods. The long synthesis time is a major drawback of ultrasound-assisted method, while the low intensity of ultrasonic devices might be the source of this limitation. In addition, three main steps of synthesis, functionalization, and later interaction of CDs with the target species make the fluorescent probes a time-consuming process. In-situ synthesis of CDs in the presence of the analyte is a smart approach to overcome this drawback [2]. Therefore, the processes of synthesis, functionalization and interaction of CDs with the target analyte would occur simultaneously.

The carbon source, passivating agent, and solvent are three main components in most synthesis routes, which could enhance the fluorescent properties. The type of solvent is a significant contributor on the surface chemical composition, morphology, and the subsequent fluorescence properties of CDs [8]. Deep eutectic solvents, as green media, could be synthesized through the formation of hydrogen bonding between a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) at a certain molar eutectic ratio [9]. Their favorable properties, including low toxicity, thermal stability, and biocompatibility are authentic proof indicating their applicability in the extraction process [10,11] and digestion of molecules [12], dispersion of solid samples [13], chromatography [14], and synthesize of CDs [15] or other nanoparticles [16,17]. Besides, deep eutectic solvents with different compositions could passivate the surface chemical composition of CDs. Therefore, they could act as a solvent and doping agent, at the same time [8]. These properties confirm CDs' potential as rapid, green, inexpensive, and homogenous assay with no need to perform any sample preparation. A simple mix and read procedure with no need to any separation and washing step are the features of the homogeneous assay [18].

Despite the potential of CDs for developing green and homogenous probes, a few publications have been reported regarding their application as a sensor in food analysis. Color additives are the most favorite agents in the food industry to attract consumer attention by stimulating and/or improving appetite. Caramel is the oldest color additive, which synthesizes by heating of carbohydrate source in the presence of reagents, including acids, alkalis, ammonium salts, and sulfites [19]. There are some molecules with low molecular weight as caramel markers generated during the caramelization process. Some caramel markers are toxic, and their presence in food and drinks could be hazardous to humans [20]. 4-methylimidazole (4-MeI) is a caramel marker and neurotoxic agent which acts as the cytochrome P450 isoenzyme inhibitor [21]. The European Commission (EC) has set a maximum of 250 mg/kg at no significant risk level for this compound [20].

Several methods have been developed to determine 4-MeI in different media (soft drink and soy sauce) based on the spectrometric and chromatographic methods such as UV, liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [22,23], liquid chromatography/tandem MS (LC/MS/MS) [24], gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [21,25,26], and gas chromatography/tandem MS (GC/MS/MS). The complexity of real samples and the size classification of such molecules are the significant contributors to reduce UV accuracy [21]. The chromatographic methods need sample preparation such as ion-pair extraction [26], solid-phase extraction (SPE) [22,24], magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) solid-phase extraction [21], dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (DLLME) [19], and headspace-solid phase microextraction (HS-SPME) [25]. In spite of the sensitivity, selectivity, and high separation ability of chromatography-mass spectrometric methods, the sample preparation is rather time-consuming and mostly costly [21]. Also, the common derivatization of 4-MeI with isobutylchloroformate followed by GC involves an extra timely step. The excess of chloroformate, not consumed in the derivatization process, also damages the inner wall coating of column during the injection process [26]. Fluorescent-based immunochromatographic (ICA) assay is another developed method for qualitative and quantitative detection of 4-MeI in which the conjugation of antibodies improves the specificity of this approach [27]. However, the physical properties such as capillary flow rate, surface area, pore size distribution, porosity, permeability, and wettability cause inconsistencies during the fabrication process. Physical and chemical modifications are also required to enhance the fluid flow, color uniformity and protein immobilization [28] while, the used antibodies or aptamers are rather expensive. To the best of our knowledge, no work has been reported based on the FCDs probe to measure 4-MeI in soft drinks.

Herein, we firstly designed a fluorescent probe based on the in-situ ultrasound-assisted formation of CDs at the presence of 4-MeI and MDES as solvent and passivating agent. The novelty of the proposed work is the in-situ synthesis of CDs chelated metals in the presence of MDES which improves the intrinsic fluorescence intensity while this phenomenon is disrupted in the presence of analyte. The specific metal-affinity interaction between the CDs-chelated metals and the imidazole ring of the analyte leads to a selective fluorescence quenching. By turning off the chelation-enhanced fluorescence of CDs-chelated metals, the imidazole-bearing analyte could be tracked. This strategy is an innovative and simple approach to develop a homogenous, sensitive, time-effective and inexpensive sensor with the minimum chemicals consumption. The investigation of the formation kinetics in the presence of 4-MeI confirms the rapidity of the prepared probe. MDES was synthesized by combining the different metal halides (ZnCl2.4H2O, CuCl2.2H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, and CoCl2.6H2O) and ethylene glycol as HBD and a quaternary ammonium salt (choline chloride or ChCl) as HBA [29–31]. Based on the reported results, we believe that the method is a homogenous, green, and rapid fluorescence assay for the detection of imidazole-based compounds.

Methanol, ethanol, sodium chloride (extra pure), citric acid anhydrous (CA), ethylene glycol (EG), zinc chloride tetrahydrate (ZnCl2.4H2O), copper (II) chloride dehydrate (CuCl2.2H2O), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2.6H2O), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2.6H2O), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO4·2H2O), potassium nitrate (KNO3), sodium benzoate (C6H5COONa) were provided from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Choline chloride (ChCl) was obtained from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium). Caffeine and 4-methylimidazole (4-MeI, 98 %), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium selenite (Na2SeO3), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), mercury(II) chloride (HgCl2), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Munich, Germany). Color additives including erythrosine, sky blue and quinoline were gifted. The stock solution of 4-MeI was prepared in ethanol at 1000 μg mL−1 and stored in the refrigerator. Working standard solutions were daily prepared by diluting the stock solution with ethanol. Soft drinks were purchased from the local market and degassed before experiments.

A Cytation 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Plate Reader (https://www.biotek.com) was used to record the fluorescence signals. For fluorescence measurements in Biotek plate reader, a black polystyrene 96-well microplate (https://www.spllifesciences.com) was utilized. To prepare multiple CDs-based fluorescence assays, under same process conditions, a Hielscher’s (UP200 St) ultrasonic lab homogenizer (200 W, 26 kHz), equipped with a VialTweeter (https://www.hielscher.com) was used. The use of VialTweeter enables the simultaneous sonication of up to 10 vials at the same intensity. A RADWAG (https://radwag.com) PS 3500.R1 Precision Balance and an 827 pH lab (https://www.metrohm.com) were employed as well.

The FTIR spectra were recorded by an ABB Bomem MB100 (Quebec, Canada), and UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained by a Pharmacia Ultrospec 3000 biotech (Massachusetts, United States). The FTIR spectrum of the aqueous solution of the synthesized CDs was obtained by a Bruker Model TENSOR II equipped with ZnSe liquid transmission cells. A Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Santa Clara, United States) was used to record the fluorescence spectra. The size distribution of CDs was measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, England). To evaluate the molecular structure of MDES and confirm the presence of metal cation, a Bruker NMR spectroscopy (Germany) was utilized.

As mentioned earlier, DESs is synthesized through the hydrogen bonding formation between HBA and HBD compounds at a certain molar eutectic ratio. The choline chloride is the non-toxic and biodegradable salt chosen as HBA compound. Then, ethylene glycol and metal salts (CuCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, and CoCl2) as HBD compounds were added to choline chloride at molar ratio eutectic ratio of 4:1:1 (EG:CuCl2:ChCl) and heated to achieve a clear liquid at 100 °C (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI and MDES medium
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 30 mg citric acid, as carbon source, and 60 mg MDES (4EG:1CuCl2:1ChCl), as both solvent and passivation agent, were prepared in 2 mL Eppendorf microtubes. Secondly, 1 mL alkaline soda sample (ammonia buffer, pH = 9), caffeine- and caramel-free, was added and vortexed for 10 s to obtain a homogenous sample. Thirdly, 4-MeI was added to Eppendorf microtubes while the concentration of 4-MeI was 240 ng mL−1 in the alkaline soda samples. Fourthly, the Eppendorf microtubes were placed in the VialTweeter of the sonication device, and the ultrasound irradiations were applied for 15 min at 30 % sonication amplitude. The ultrasound irradiations changed the color of samples from royal blue to brown, which confirms the CDs formation. Finally, an aliquot of 100 μL of the synthesized CDs was added to the black polystyrene 96-well microplate and placed onto the Multi-Mode plate Reader to measure the fluorescence signal. The in-situ synthesizing process and the rapid detection of 4-MeI are schematically shown in Fig. 2 . The blank soda samples, with no 4-MeI spiking, were prepared and examined prior to each experiment. In all experiments, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity (I0) is related to the blank samples and expressed by relative fluorescence unit (RFU). The analytical response (I0/I) is contributed to ratio of the intrinsic fluorescence intensity to the fluorescence intensity (I) at the presence of 4-MeI. In all figures, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity and the analytical response are shown by dotted lines and bar charts, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI and MDES
ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, the ultrasound-assisted technique was utilized to in-situ synthesize CDs in the presence of 4-MeI. This protocol significantly improved the method rapidity and sensitivity. The simultaneous implementation of MDES as a passivation agent and solvent is also an innovative approach leading to time- and cost-effectiveness and reduction of chemicals consumption.

To confirm the presence of metal cation in MDES, the H-NMR spectra for Cu-MDES and DES without copper (II) (4EG:1ChCl) (green and red lines in Fig. 3 a, respectively) were obtained. The characteristic chemical shifts are assigned to the N+(CH3)3 (3.1 ppm, peak 1), CH2-O and CH2-N+ (4.39 ppm, peak 2), and OH in choline chloride (5.25 ppm, peak 3). The odd electron density of copper (ii) leads to the chemical shifts of the lower fields, as shown in Fig. 3a (green). In addition, the line broadening might be caused by slow relaxing of copper ions [32] or fast exchange between the different non-specific binding sites for divalent metal ions [33].

The formation of the hydrogen bonds between choline chloride and ethylene glycol is the main reason for the MDES formation. Therefore, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding between ethylene glycol molecules decreases in the presence of ChCl, and the stretching vibration of the OH group shifts to higher wavenumbers for Cu-DES [34]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the stretching vibration of the OH group in ChCl, EG, and Cu-DES appears at 3220, 3343, and 3370 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic band at 953 cm−1 for Cu-DES is related to the C–N+ stretching originated from ChCl. These results confirm the successful formation of MDES.

The composition of the synthesized CDs was also characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the peaks at 3460, 1702, and 1085 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH, CO, and CO groups, respectively. The CN stretching and NH bending vibrations appeared at 1045 and 1570 cm−1. These peaks confirm the presence of carboxyl, alcohol, and amine functional groups on the surface of CDs. The stretching vibration of C–Cl at 879 cm−1 is also assigned to the doping chloride ion originated from ChCl. The peaks at low frequencies below 700 cm−1 are related to Cu-O vibrations [35]. The stretching and bending vibrations of CH are related to the peaks at 2982 and 1453 cm−1. The origination of the functional groups on the CDs’ surface from the used carbon source and solvent in relevant to the published references and our initiative goal is consistent with the doping Cl on CDs (879 cm−1) and the presence of chlorine in Cu-DES at 884 cm−1. Also, the origination of doping CO on CDs (1702 cm−1) is accompanied with the presence of carboxyl group in citric acid at 1745 and 1702 cm−1.

The size distribution of CDs was also investigated and as shown in Fig. 4b, the synthesized CDs have a nano-scale size. Their particle size shows a narrow distribution in the range of 6−8 nm with an average size of ∼7.2 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded in the 230−600 nm interval and depicted in Fig. 4c. The strong peak at 330 nm might be assigned to the n-π* electronic transition of the CO bond. The photoluminescence properties of CDs were also investigated, and the excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 4c. The spectra exhibit the optimal emission peak at 420 nm at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm. Therefore, the prepared CDs emit a strong blue fluorescence under UV lamp excitation (330 nm). The emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths are depicted in Fig. 4d. The excitation-independent emission of CDs confirms the uniform and relatively narrow distribution of the surface states of the activated groups on CDs [36]. The red-shift was observed by increasing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 370 nm.
The influencing parameters on the experimental process were optimized to improve the fluorescence signals and achieve the highest possible sensitivity and subsequent applicability.

Surface chemical passivation and functionalization are strongly associated with the applications and properties of CDs [37]. Considering the type of application, a passivation agent is mostly employed for the surface modification of CDs. Implementation of DES as low-cost and green solvents in the CDs synthesis process is the innovational approach with no need to any doping agent addition. The superiority of DESs as both solvent and doping agent was established by their remarkable properties, including low toxicity, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [8]. To investigate the effect of the solvent type and the surface modification on the fluorescence signal, four MDESs with different metal salts (CuCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, and CoCl2), DES without metal salt (4EG:1ChCl), alcohol (methanol and ethanol), water and soda sample without addition of citric acid and solvent (just soda) were selected. The obtained results show the intrinsic fluorescence intensity (I0) was enhanced accordingly, MDES (64 × 103) > DES (18 × 103) > ethanol (15 × 103) ≈ methanol (14.9 × 103) > water (9 × 103) >>> just soda (1.7 × 103) (Fig. 5 a, dotted lines). The chelating metal ions on the CDs surface has a significant role on the signal improvement which more probably is due to chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [38]. In addition, the analytical responses (I0/I) are remarkably increased in MDES (Fig. 5a, bars), confirming the occurrence of maximum fluorescence quenching. Finally, CuCl2 was chosen as the optimum metal salt for the preparation of MDES.

To investigate the effect of citric acid quantity, various citric acid amounts (15−60 mg) were selected. As shown in Fig. 5b, this parameter virtually affects the intrinsic fluorescence intensity (I0) and, consequently, the analytical response (I0/I). The observed signals remained almost constant within the 30−45 mg range and reduced at 60 mg. An amount of 30 mg was therefore selected as the optimum citric acid quantity. The intrinsic fluorescence intensity of CDs strongly depends on the carbon precursor/passivation agent ratio [39].

The carbohydrates in soda samples could be the carbon source for CDs formation. Therefore, a 1 mL of soda sample without citric acid, MDES, and 4-MeI was sonicated for 15 min at 30 % of sonication amplitude (Fig. 5a, Just soda label). The intrinsic fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs is negligible relative to the intensity of CDs obtained from citric acid.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence property of CDs is studied by several other works. Accordingly, the basic media is essential for formation of CDs via the ultrasonic-assisted method [2]. By implementing different pH values (4–12), as shown in Fig. 5c, pH = 9 is the most suitable value for the measurement of 4-MeI.

As mentioned earlier, the carbon precursor/passivation agent ratio directly affects the intrinsic fluorescence intensity. Besides, the current sensor is designed based on the interaction of copper ions chelation on the surface of CDs with the imidazole ring of 4-MeI. Therefore, the amount of passivation agent should be optimized. The obtained data for different amounts of MDES is shown in Fig. 5d, and the quantity of 60 mg appears to be the optimum value.

The sonication amplitude is an influential and device-dependent variable which should be optimized in the ultrasound-assisted protocols. Sonication amplitude and intensity have a direct relationship, and implementing a low amplitude setting leads to low-intensity sonication. By increasing the amplitude setting, cavitation intensity within the sample is also increased which is accompanied by decreasing the particle size [40]. According to the results in Fig. 5e, the highest analytical response was obtained at 30 % sonication amplitude. At higher sonication amplitudes 4-MeI (as carbon source) is possibly converted into CDs nanoparticles and the analytical response is therefore decreased.

The sonication time was considered in the range of 10−25 min interval and according to Fig. 5f, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity and also the analytical response is enhanced as the time is enhanced. After 15 min, the signals remained almost constant and a duration time of 15 min was chosen as the optimum sonication time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation kinetics of CDs in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The formation kinetic of CDs in different solvents (Cu-MDESs, ethanol, and water) versus different sonication times was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 (a–c, dotted), the highest intrinsic fluorescence intensity of CDs was obtained in Cu-MDES media. The chelation of CDs by metal cations is probably leads to the improved fluorescence intensity. Also, the analytical responses (I0/I) in different media were studied at different sonication times. The results in Fig. 6 (a–c, bar) confirm that the analytical responses are enhanced by implementing Cu-MDES as solvent and doping agent. The synthesis of CDs in MDES occurs in a relatively shorter sonication time, confirming the superiority of the designed sensor as a rapid fluorescence assay. Under continuous irradiation, the sound absorption capability decreases in the lower-density media [41]. Deep eutectic solvents with higher densities than water and organic solvents have greater sound absorption capability. Therefore, by increasing the medium density in the presence of MDES and sound absorption capability, CDs are expected to be synthesized in shorter time.

A wide range of ions was tested to evaluate the interference of different ions on the analytical response. The final concentration of 4-MeI and possible interference species was 240 ng mL−1 and 24 μg mL−1 (100 folds) for each selectivity test, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6d, an intensive signal is observed for 4-MeI, while the responses of other interference species are rather insignificant. The analytical response at the presence of caffeine (24 μg mL−1), as a highly possible coexisting compound, was led to a considerable signal (Fig. 6d). This result was predictable due to the presence of the imidazole ring in caffeine. For all experiments related to the real samples analysis, the caffeine-free sodas were used to detect 4-MeI.

The formation kinetics of CDs in Cu-MDESs, ethanol, and water at the presence of caffeine were also investigated while the final concentration of caffeine was set as 240 ng mL−1 (similar to 4-MeI). In contrary with 4-MeI, a different kinetic pattern is observed and the analytical responses at the presence of caffeine are decreased by increasing the sonication time (Fig. 6e–g). According to the FT-IR studies of synthesized CDs at the presence of caffeine under extended sonication time, caffeine is consumed as carbon source. By increasing the sonication time, the possibility of implementation of target molecules with the higher carbon numbers as carbon source is enhanced. The extended sonication time apparently leads to structure degradation, more CDs quantity and subsequent fluorescence emission. According to this contrary result, the detection of 4-MeI at the presence of caffeine can be proceed after 25 min.

Quinoline yellow is another color additive in orange soft drinks with imidazole-like nitrogen atom. The obtained data (Fig. 6d) represent the applicability of the developed sensor for detection of quinolone, as well.

Coordination of a molecular fluorophore (as ligand) to non-quenching metals, i.e. Zn(II), Cd(II) and sometimes Cu(II) leads to the formation of metal complexes with enhanced fluorescence via chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF). The CHEF phenomenon decreases the rate of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and subsequently enhances the radiative pathway [42,43]. There are some reports which confirm the affinity and stability of di-positive metal ions to form the complexes with humic, benzoic and salicylic acids. Humic acids macromolecules possess O-donor binding sites (as major binding sites) to form the complexes. The interaction strengths of d-metals followed the Irving–Williams order: Co (II)< Ni (II)< Cu (II)> Zn (II) [44].

According to the obtained FT-IR results, the synthesized CDs were functionalized with oxygen-rich groups such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl. Therefore, it could be assumed that their interactions with the divalent metals occur in a similar fashion with humic acids macromolecules. The enhancement of intrinsic fluorescence of CDs in MDES versus DES might be related to the coordination of oxygen-containing groups of CDs to metal ions. The chelation process, more probably, leads to a more rigid structure, preventing the collisions among CDs and consequently is associated with the fluorescence emission enhancement. Considering the data illustrated in Fig. 5a (dotted line) and Irving–Williams order, the CHEF phenomenon at the presence of Cu2+ is encouraged.

In this research, the specific metal-affinity interactions between the imidazole ring and metal ions lead to fluorescence quenching. A donor pyridine-like nitrogen atom in the imidazole ring induces the selectivity binding to metal cations [45] while, transition metals such as Cu2 +, Ni2 + and Zn2 + have preference for nitrogen-containing side-chains [46]. Therefore, the imidazole ring binding to the metal ion on the CDs surface inhibits the CHEF between CDs and consequently causes the quenching of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7 ). The affinity order of divalent transition metal ions to imidazole ring is different in immobilized metal affinity chromatography (IMAC), and the usual affinity order is as follows: Cu (II)> Ni (II)> Zn (II)> Co (II) [47]. Different analytical responses (I0/I), observed in various MDES might be related to this affinity order.

The synthesis of CDs in the presence of 4-MeI causes significant fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching is described by Stern-Volmer equation (Eq 1):
(1) I0/I = Ksv [Q] + C
The optimum condition for the developed sensor is: 30 mg citric acid, 60 mg Cu-MDES, pH = 9, 30 % sonication amplitude, and 15 min sonication time.

A homogeneous, green, rapid, and miniaturized fluorescence probe was developed to characterize and determine imidazole-based compounds. The synthesis, doping, and sensing processes were designed simultaneously to achieve a time effective homogeneous assay with reduced chemical consumption. Carbon dots were synthesized in metal deep eutectic solvent (as solvent and doping agent) and presence of the analyte. The strong interaction between the imidazole ring of the analyte and metal-chelated on carbon dots causes fluorescence quenching. By adopting the in-situ synthesis, doping, and sensing processes, the need for performing sample preparation was omitted. The results of the formation kinetics confirm the rapidity of the developed probe. In addition to the satisfactory characterization results, the analytical data are also another authentic proof indicating convenient preparation and application of suggested CDs-based sensor to imidazole-based compounds detection. The simultaneous presence of two or more analyte targets with imidazole-ring in the sample might limit the applicability of sensor. Depending on the number of carbon and size of analyte, by adjusting the sonication time and amplitude and considering the relevant kinetic formation, the desired analytes could be tracked without the interferences from others. Moreover, the chemometric-based approaches could be beneficial to resolve this limitation.
Faranak Manshaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Habib Bagheri: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Ali Es-haghi: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Research Council (Grant number QA990417) and Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology (SUT) and Razi Vaccine & Serum Research Institute are acknowledged for supporting this project.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Faranak Manshaei is a Ph.D. candidate at the Department of chemistry, Sharif University of Technology. She received her BS degree in Pure Chemistry from the Isfahan University of Technology in 2015. In 2017 she became a graduate student in Professor Habib Bagheri’s laboratory at the Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology. Her research interests span fields of nanotechnology, electrospun nanofibers, fluorescence sensors and platforms with focus on design and preparation of carbon dot based tools for drug and carcinogens detection.

Habib Bagheri received his BS degree in Applied Chemistry from Sharif University of Technology in 1985, his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from University of East Anglia, Norwich, England in 1991, and his postdoctoral training from the Vrije Universiteit, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Amsterdam. In 1993, he became a faculty member in Sharif University of Technology, Tehran. Currently he is a distinguished Professor and his research is focused on environmental- and bio-analysis, extractive phases, microfluidic devices and fluorescence sensors. He has authored over 130 papers and four book chapters.

Ali Es-haghi is an associate professor in Razi Vaccine and Serum Research Institute. He obtained his BS degree in Applied Chemistry from University of Guilan in 1998, his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Sharif University of Technology in 2007. In 2014, he became the head of Physico Chemistry Department of Razi Vaccine and Serum Research Institute. His research is focused on environmental- and bio-analysis and extractive phases. He has authored or co-authored over 50 papers.
